nodes.title: Check only own keys before creating bucket
nodes.why: This avoids collisions with inherited object properties and keeps grouping safe for edge keys.
nodes.tradeoff: More verbose, but safer and more explicit.

nodes.title: Create bucket only when key appears
nodes.why: We allocate arrays only for keys that actually exist in the input.
nodes.alternative: Pre-build all possible keys before looping.
nodes.tradeoff: Lazy init is efficient and simple, but requires one existence check per item.

nodes.title: Push items to preserve input order
nodes.why: Items stay in the same order they appeared in the original array.
nodes.alternative: Insert at the front or sort each bucket later.
nodes.tradeoff: Push is fastest and clear, but it does not apply custom ordering.

nodes.title: Use reduce as single-pass accumulator
nodes.why: `reduce` keeps the whole grouping flow in one expression while still doing one pass.
nodes.alternative: Use a `for...of` loop with a mutable result object.
nodes.tradeoff: Concise style, but some readers find loop-based code easier to scan.

nodes.title: Initialize and push in one line
nodes.why: This line creates the bucket when needed and appends the item immediately.
nodes.tradeoff: Very compact, but relies on newer syntax some users may not know well.

nodes.title: Return accumulator from reducer
nodes.why: Reduce needs the accumulator returned each iteration to keep building the grouped result.
nodes.alternative: Mutate an external object outside reduce.
nodes.tradeoff: Functional pattern is neat, but forgetting this return breaks the logic.

nodes.title: Use Map for grouping state
nodes.why: Map avoids prototype-key pitfalls and handles key lookup cleanly.
nodes.alternative: Use a plain object from the start.
nodes.tradeoff: Safer key handling, but requires conversion at the end.

nodes.title: Create map bucket on first key hit
nodes.why: Each key needs an array before we can push items into it.
nodes.alternative: Read with `map.get(key) ?? []` and set back every time.
nodes.tradeoff: Two-step check is straightforward, though slightly more verbose.

nodes.title: Convert Map back to plain object
nodes.why: The exercise expects an object result, so we convert map entries at the end.
nodes.alternative: Return the map directly and let callers consume map APIs.
nodes.tradeoff: Matches expected output, but key identity is stringified in the final object.
